question_text: A puddle of water gradually disappears on a sunny day without boiling. Separately, a kettle of water reaches 100°C and bubbles vigorously. Compare these two processes in terms of: (a) the name of each process, (b) where particles escape from, and (c) the energy required per process. Explain why one process is faster than the other.
correct_answer: (a) Puddle = evaporation; kettle = boiling. (b) Evaporation occurs from the surface only; boiling occurs throughout the liquid. (c) Both require latent heat of vaporization, but boiling requires more total energy because it happens faster and at a specific temperature (100°C). Boiling is faster because heat energy is supplied more rapidly by the kettle, and bubbles form throughout the liquid allowing faster escape of particles.
explanation: Evaporation happens slowly from the surface when particles gain enough energy to escape as gas; boiling is the rapid, vigorous version that occurs at 100°C when energy is added quickly. Boiling is faster because the entire liquid is heated, not just the surface, so more particles can escape simultaneously as bubbles.
